description: Hand-curated multi-step REAL decision sequences for the closed-loop trajectory benchmark. Per case: several actors, several ordered decision steps; each step freezes the world at its own as_of, adds only evidence public BEFORE that as_of, and labels the actor's real next action. Branch worlds persist across steps (state, memories, executed actions), so this measures sequences, reaction anticipation, and error compounding — not isolated decisions. Same contamination caveat as the single-decision corpus: famous sequences; architecture deltas (persistent vs stateless) are the contamination-controlled signal.
leakage_rule: every evidence date <= the consuming step's as_of; a step's situation text never reveals that step's or later steps' outcomes; labels never enter prompts.

commitments.statement: Publicly demanded removal of the missiles

base_evidence.text: US reconnaissance discovered Soviet missile sites under construction in Cuba (known to the US leadership; public from Oct 22).
Kennedy announced a naval quarantine of Cuba and demanded removal of Soviet missiles.

steps.situation: The quarantine line is now active; your ships approach it and the world watches whether you submit to inspection or defy the blockade.

evidence_additions.text: Khrushchev publicly denounced the quarantine as piracy and an act of aggression.
Several Soviet ships slowed or altered course near the quarantine line; tankers continued.

steps.source_note: Khrushchev publicly defied the quarantine while quietly keeping missile-carrying ships back (Oct 24-25).

steps.situation: The blockade holds, US invasion preparations are visible, and no diplomatic exit exists yet. You must decide whether to open one.

evidence_additions.text: At the UN, Stevenson confronted the Soviet ambassador with photographic evidence of the missile sites.
US low-level reconnaissance intensified; invasion staging in Florida was widely reported.

steps.source_note: Khrushchev's private first letter of Oct 26 offered withdrawal in exchange for a US non-invasion pledge.

steps.situation: You hold two Soviet letters: a private conciliatory one offering withdrawal for a non-invasion pledge, and a harder public one demanding Turkey missiles too. A U-2 has just been shot down over Cuba; your generals urge striking.

evidence_additions.text: Khrushchev's private letter offered withdrawal in exchange for a US non-invasion pledge.
A second, public Khrushchev letter demanded US Jupiter missiles leave Turkey as part of any deal.
A U-2 was shot down over Cuba; the pilot was killed; the military pressed for retaliation.

steps.source_note: Kennedy accepted the FIRST letter's terms publicly (Trollope ploy) while privately conceding Turkey via RFK, Oct 27 night.

steps.situation: Kennedy has publicly accepted your first letter's framework — non-invasion pledge for withdrawal — and his brother privately conveyed that the Turkey missiles will quietly leave later. Time to answer, with the military machinery on both sides still in motion.

evidence_additions.text: Robert Kennedy privately conveyed to the Soviet ambassador that Jupiter missiles would be removed later but never as a public trade, and that time was nearly out.

steps.source_note: Radio Moscow announced the withdrawal on Oct 28.

commitments.statement: Signed merger agreement at $54.20 with specific performance clause

goals: protect the company through litigation if needed

base_evidence.text: Musk offered to buy Twitter at $54.20 per share, calling it his best and final offer.
Musk disclosed committed financing of about $46.5 billion for the bid.

steps.situation: The financed $54.20 offer stands at a large premium; the poison pill is in place but institutional shareholders press for engagement. You must respond to the offer.

evidence_additions.text: The board adopted a poison pill in response to the unsolicited offer.
Reports indicated the board re-engaged seriously with Musk after his financing was committed.

steps.source_note: The board unanimously accepted the $44B agreement on 2022-04-25.

steps.situation: Markets have fallen since April; tech valuations are down sharply; your team claims Twitter's spam-account disclosures are inadequate and the data you demanded is incomplete. The agreement binds you at $54.20 with specific performance.

evidence_additions.text: Musk tweeted the deal was 'temporarily on hold' pending spam-account details.
Musk's lawyers accused Twitter of breaching the agreement by withholding bot data.

steps.source_note: Musk filed to terminate the merger agreement on 2022-07-08.

steps.situation: The buyer has filed to terminate the signed agreement. Your stock is far below the deal price. Delaware specific-performance precedent appears favorable. You must respond.

evidence_additions.text: Musk's lawyers filed notice terminating the $44B agreement over bot-count disputes.
The board chair publicly stated the board intended to close the transaction on the agreed terms.

steps.source_note: Twitter sued Musk in Delaware Chancery on 2022-07-12 seeking specific performance.

steps.situation: Trial begins October 17. Discovery has gone badly for you — your own texts are public, the judge has signaled skepticism of the bot defense, and your deposition is imminent. Financing remains committed at $54.20.

evidence_additions.text: Chancellor McCormick largely sided with Twitter in discovery rulings; trial set for October 17.
Embarrassing private texts about the deal were released in court filings.

steps.source_note: On 2022-10-04 Musk proposed closing at the original $54.20; the deal closed Oct 27.

commitments.statement: Told colleagues he had a mandate of 14 million votes and would not resign

base_evidence.text: Downing Street's account of what the PM knew about the Pincher allegations shifted repeatedly.
Chancellor Sunak and Health Secretary Javid resigned within minutes of each other.

steps.situation: Your chancellor and health secretary resigned last night; junior resignations are accelerating through the day. You must decide whether to fight on or stand down.

evidence_additions.text: Johnson appointed Zahawi chancellor within hours and vowed to carry on.
Dozens of ministers and aides resigned through the day; replacements grew hard to find.

steps.source_note: Johnson publicly vowed to fight on through July 6, telling the Commons he would keep going.

steps.situation: The premiership is visibly collapsing but the PM refuses to go. Senior ministers must decide whether to confront him collectively tonight.

evidence_additions.text: A delegation of cabinet ministers gathered in Downing Street awaiting the PM.

steps.source_note: The cabinet delegation told Johnson on the evening of July 6 that his position was unrecoverable.

steps.situation: Your cabinet told you to your face the position is unrecoverable; you sacked Gove overnight; the 1922 Committee is moving to change no-confidence rules; over fifty ministers have gone. Morning has come.

evidence_additions.text: Johnson dismissed Michael Gove, who had told him to resign.
Resignations passed fifty; the 1922 executive prepared rule changes enabling a fresh confidence vote.

steps.source_note: Johnson announced his resignation as party leader outside No. 10 on the morning of July 7.
